CLINICAL INDICATION: Acute onset chest pain and shortness of breath.

TECHNIQUE: Single frontal view of the chest, portable AP projection.

FINDINGS:
The lungs are partially visualized. There is a large right-sided pneumothorax with complete collapse of the right lung, which is retracted toward the right hilum. A visceral pleural line is clearly identified. The right hemithorax is otherwise hyperlucent without visible lung markings peripherally. The trachea is midline. The mediastinum does not appear significantly shifted, though the left lung is hyperinflated, likely reflecting compensatory changes. No subcutaneous emphysema is identified along the chest wall. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits in size. The visualized osseous structures are intact. No rib fractures are identified.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large right-sided pneumothorax with complete collapse of the right lung. Urgent clinical correlation and chest tube placement are recommended.
2. No contralateral pneumothorax. No significant mediastinal shift on this single view, though tension physiology cannot be excluded clinically and close monitoring is advised.
